questions.text: When he believes he is being freed from a worldly occupation and redirected toward God, how is he most likely to respond emotionally and spiritually?
questions.held_out_passage: Now was the day come wherein I was in deed to be freed of my Rhetoric Professorship, whereof in thought I was already freed. And it was done. Thou didst rescue my tongue, whence Thou hadst before rescued my heart. And I blessed Thee, rejoicing; retiring with all mine to the villa.

questions.text: If he enters a quieter period after leaving behind prideful public work, what kind of use is he most likely to make of his writing?

questions.text: How does he tend to interpret inner correction and the humbling of his former self-exaltation?
questions.held_out_passage: For my remembrance recalls me, and pleasant is it to me, O Lord, to confess to Thee, by what inward goads Thou tamedst me; and how Thou hast evened me, lowering the mountains and hills of my high imaginations, straightening my crookedness, and smoothing my rough ways;

questions.text: When someone close to him resists explicitly Christian expression in shared intellectual work, what outcome would he welcome?
questions.held_out_passage: and how Thou also subduedst the brother of my heart, Alypius, unto the name of Thy Only Begotten, our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ, which he would not at first vouchsafe to have inserted in our writings. For rather would he have them savour of the lofty cedars of the Schools, which the Lord hath now broken down, than of the wholesome herbs of the Church, the antidote against serpents.

questions.text: What effect do sacred songs and prayers most likely have on him when he reads them in a state of new devotion?
questions.held_out_passage: Oh, in what accents spake I unto Thee, my God, when I read the Psalms of David, those faithful songs, and sounds of devotion, which allow of no swelling spirit, as yet a Catechumen, and a novice in Thy real love, resting in that villa, with Alypius a Catechumen, my mother cleaving to us, in female garb with masculine faith, with the tranquillity of age, motherly love, Christian piety! Oh, what accents did I utter unto Thee in those Psalms, and how was I by them kindled towards Thee, and on fire to rehearse them, if possible, through the whole world, against the pride of mankind!

questions.text: How is he likely to react toward people he believes are trapped in serious religious error?
questions.held_out_passage: With what vehement and bitter sorrow was I angered at the Manichees! and again I pitied them, for they knew not those Sacraments, those medicines, and were mad against the antidote which might have recovered them of their madness.

questions.text: If he wants opponents to encounter his deepest convictions authentically, under what conditions would he prefer they hear him?
questions.held_out_passage: How I would they had then been somewhere near me, and without my knowing that they were there, could have beheld my countenance, and heard my words, when I read the fourth Psalm in that time of my rest, and how that Psalm wrought upon me: When I called, the God of my righteousness heard me; in tribulation Thou enlargedst me. Have mercy upon me, O Lord, and hear my prayer. Would that what I uttered on these words, they could hear, without my knowing whether they heard, lest they should think I spake it for their sakes! Because in truth neither should I speak the same things, nor in the same way, if I perceived that they heard and saw me; nor if I spake them would they so receive them, as when I spake by and for myself before Thee, out of the natural feelings of my soul.

questions.text: When confronted by words that expose his former errors, what mixed emotional response is he most likely to experience before God?

For I had loved vanity, and sought after leasing.

questions.text: How does he interpret his past attachment to falsehood once he comes to believe divine truth has been revealed?
questions.held_out_passage: He cries out, How long? He cries out, Know this: and I so long, not knowing, loved vanity, and sought after leasing: and therefore I heard and trembled, because it was spoken unto such as I remembered myself to have been. For in those phantoms which I had held for truths, was there vanity and leasing;

questions.held_out_passage: I further read, Be angry, and sin not. And how was I moved, O my God, who had now learned to be angry at myself for things past, that I might not sin in time to come!

questions.text: Would he hold that a person cannot even search for what has been completely erased from memory?
